name: Inventory Manager
scenario: You're building the backend logic for an inventory management system used by a small e-commerce store. The store has 15 products, each with: a product ID, current stock level, and price. The owner needs to: see all products, find products with low stock (need reordering), search by ID, sort by various criteria (price, stock), and identify the most/least expensive items. This is real warehouse logic — the kind that runs in Flipkart's seller hub, Amazon's vendor portal, or any e-commerce backend.
description: Build an `InventoryManager` class with methods for inventory queries and reports. Uses parallel arrays (product IDs, stocks, prices) — a stepping stone before learning objects in Module 4. Practices the full toolkit from this topic: Arrays utility methods, custom search/sort, in-place vs new-array operations, two-pointer technique.

core.description: Three parallel arrays at class-level: productIds (int[]), stocks (int[]), prices (double[]) — at least 10 products
`printInventory()` method — uses Arrays.toString to display all arrays
`findById(int targetId)` method — uses linear search, returns INDEX of matching product or -1
`countLowStock(int threshold)` method — returns count of products with stock <= threshold
`getSortedPrices()` method — returns NEW array with prices sorted ascending (original unchanged)
`getMostExpensivePrice()` method — returns highest price (don't modify the array)
main() demonstrates ALL methods with a complete inventory report

bonus.description: Add `getTotalInventoryValue()` — sum of (stock × price) for all products
Add `findCheapestProductId()` — find product ID of cheapest product (must track index of min)
Add `getProductIdsWithLowStock(int threshold)` — returns NEW array of product IDs (just IDs) that need reorder
Add `binarySearchProductId(int[] sortedIds, int targetId)` — implement binary search for product IDs (after sorting them)

data_to_use:
Use these 12 products:

```java
static int[] productIds = {101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108, 109, 110, 111, 112};
static int[] stocks =     {  45,  12,  67,   3,  28,  91,   8,  53,  19,   2,  74,  35};
static double[] prices =  {249.99, 1499.0, 89.50, 349.0, 599.99, 19.99, 1999.0, 449.99, 79.99, 2999.0, 124.50, 199.0};
```

Expected results:
- Most expensive: 2999.0 (Product 110)
- Cheapest: 19.99 (Product 106)
- Low stock (<= 10): Products 104, 107, 110 (count: 3)
- Total inventory value: sum of stock × price for all 12

criteria: findById linear search with -1 sentinel (15 pts)
getSortedPrices returns new sorted array, original unchanged (15 pts)
Output is clear and well-formatted (5 pts)
Uses Arrays utility methods appropriately (5 pts)
No modification of class-level arrays where 'new' is expected (5 pts)
Low stock IDs returned as new array (20 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 AI hints. You have all the patterns from the topic — search, sort, copy, find extreme with index, iterate parallel arrays. The trickiest pieces are: parallel array iteration (index i in productIds matches index i in stocks/prices) and getSortedPrices NOT modifying the original (must copy first).
submission_instructions: Save as InventoryManager.java. Run it. Verify the original arrays are unchanged after running getSortedPrices. Push to GitHub portfolio — this is foundational e-commerce backend logic.
tips_for_success: Parallel arrays: think of them as columns of a spreadsheet. Row i has matching info in all three arrays.
When sorting prices, COPY first, then sort the copy. Use Arrays.copyOf(prices, prices.length).
For findById: linear search through productIds. Return the INDEX (not the ID).
For most expensive: just iterate prices, track max. Don't sort — that's overkill.
For cheapest product ID: track index of minimum price, then look up productIds[that index].
For low stock IDs (bonus): two-pass approach OK — first count, then create array of right size and fill it. Or use a generous array and trim with copyOf.
concepts_reinforced: Arrays.sort modifies in-place — use on copies when needed
Parallel arrays as a pre-OOP pattern
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this: Beyond practicing array operations, you experience PARALLEL ARRAYS — a pattern that predates object-oriented programming. In real Java code (Module 4+), you'd use a Product CLASS to bundle id/stock/price together. But seeing the parallel-arrays approach makes you appreciate why OOP exists: keeping related data in sync across multiple arrays is error-prone. This project intentionally feels a bit clunky in that respect — that's the lesson that motivates OOP. By the time you're learning classes next module, you'll viscerally understand why they're an improvement.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Add a method to UPDATE stock for a product by ID (find + modify)
Add a method to find the product with the BEST stock-to-price ratio (value)
Implement insertion of a new product (requires creating larger arrays — preview of ArrayList motivation)
Sort products by price (must sort all three arrays in sync — tricky! This is the OOP motivation)
Add a 'restock recommendation' that calculates how many units of each low-stock item to order based on average demand
